Step 1: 	Snap Suggestion 	Part 1
ALL CONTENT © COPYRIGHT 2011 AND BEYOND | JON BENSON 3X
FORMULAS, INC. | ALL RIGHTS RESERVED | NO DUPLICATION ALLOWED
Jon: 	Welcome to the tutorial presentation on step one of 5. This is the 5-
Step VSL process and step one is called the snap suggestion.
Now, let’s start with some tips before I define what snap suggestion
means.
When creating a winning video sales letter, it pays to format your text
using the title case technique that we covered in the 3X VSL Toolbox.
This creates the appearance of headlines.
Title case is exactly what you see in title case technique. It’s just
capitalizing the first letter of the word.
So, here’s the thing: you need to test this. You want to make sure that
you make 2 copies of your video sales letter, if you want to test it, I
recommend that you do, one with title case, and one with normal case.
Usually, title case performs better; so if you just wanted to go with title
case, you could do that. But we always recommend testing.
Now, we’ve given you a really cool website that will make it very easy
to do this. This will allow you to type out everything that you want to
type, in normal case, which you’ve probably already done in your Word
documents, etc., in your text editor. Then, you can just cut-and-paste
into this website.
Don’t go there right now; please stay with me.
But it’s TinyURL.com/MakeTitleCase. It’s really cool. You just click a
button and everything becomes title case, so you don’t have to go
through your entire VSL and your entire Word document and capitalize
the first letter of every word. That would be a real pain. So, you don’t

-- 1 of 12 --

2
have to do that.
Okay, the next tip I want to give you is called an action drop. Now
what is an action drop? It’s something that you’re going to be using
throughout your entire video sales letter. And this is a technique that I
created to push the reader forward visually, and push them forward
from an NLP, or Neuro-Linguistic Programming perspective.
In other words, you want to keep pushing your user and your viewer
forward; forward through the video to continue watching, forward to
take action toward the end of this. And this is all related around what I
call the action drop.
This is what it is. You drop a hint to bring the reader’s focus back into
the present moment. And you do that by using words to prompt the
user to action within the moment.
Wow. What do I mean by that? It sounds kind of complicated. Well,
it’s actually so easy.
Look at these words here. Today. Now. You see. Listen. Actually.
All of these are action drops, and they will appear – most of the time –
at the beginning of a sentence.
So, while you might want to begin a sentence by saying something
like, “You’re going to find out how to blank,” you can make that an
action drop sentence by simply saying, “Today, you are going to find
out blank.” And what that word does is it triggers immediate action.
Today, now, see – you’re actually suggesting that they see something.
Listen. Actually. These all are moments. These are all present
moment action words. So, we’re going to use them throughout your
VSL, and I’m going to show you how.
So, let me give you a few quick examples. “I have to warn you first,”
versus “Now, I have to warn first.”
What does the word now do to this sentence? Now is immediate.
Now implants a command. And this is what you want to do. You’re
implanting a command into the subconscious and into the conscious
mind to say, “Now, pay attention now. Listen to what I’m saying now.”
It’s a very powerful word. So, just adding this before a sentence can
have a lot of effect.
Now, here’s another example for you. “You know you need to plan, if
you really want to make more money online.” Okay, I’m just making
this up; somebody has a make money online product, let’s say. Well,
this is another way to do that. “Listen, you know you need to plan, if
you really want to make more money online.” And if you’re listening to

-- 2 of 12 --

3
how I’m talking, that’s very similar to how we’re going to teach you to
talk in the later videos. It’s voice intonation issues and spacing and all
this stuff that I’m going to cover later.
But when I say, “Listen,” you have a tendency to listen when there’s a
space after the word listen, especially. And you’ll learn all these
techniques in the later videos. But just putting that one word into that
sentence makes a huge difference.
I want to caution you to avoid the word “but.” Avoid the word “but.”
And the reason why is it’s called a stop term. In Neuro-Linguistic
Programming, a stop term means it halts the reader in the moment.
And it can be a good thing, if you know exactly where to use it. But
there are far better words to use where you don’t have to worry about
accidentally stopping them and having them freeze. And usually at this
kind of a stop term, it’s a negative thing. It says no or it says stop in a
negative way.
So, here’s the words I use instead. I use yet, however, and and. Now,
this is not ideal grammar, and it does not matter. This course is not
about creating a VSL that has perfect grammar in it. This course is
about creating a VSL that makes you money. And this is the stuff that
works. I’m going to give you some examples on how to use that.
So, instead of saying, “But instead, I want you to imagine,” you would
say, “However, instead I want you to imagine.” And you can kind of
see and feel how the word “but” in that sentence slows things down. It
kind of creates some drag, some friction. And that’s why the word
“however” is so much more powerful.
However is also an imagination term. It’s a long story on why that is
imagination term, but it is, and that provokes the reader’s imagination.
That’s good.
Here’s another example. “This has all the advantages, but without…”
And I just change this to, “This has all the advantages, and without…”
The power of “and” here is it’s an addition statement. So in other
words “but without” is an exclusion statement. You’re excluding
something. “But without.” It’s a negative way of looking at this.
When I say, “and without,” it’s just like saying “and you get this, and
you get this, and you get…”
People are hearing this and they’re going, “Oh, there’s more. I get
more.” “This has all the advantages and without…” It’s pretty cool.
Now we’re going to go over another tip here, and that’s avoid the

-- 3 of 12 --

4
assumption.
In a few of the nightmare stories that I’ve read from clients in the past,
a lot of clients would write things like, “I know what you may be
thinking,” and things of that nature. Well, you don’t know what your
reader is thinking. And suggesting that you do can come off as a tad
insulting, on the psychological level, and you don’t want to do that.
So, when you say, “I know what you are thinking,” you risk 2 things.
Number one, the viewer wasn’t thinking about anything related to this,
wasn’t even thinking about it. But now, he or she is thinking about it
because you’ve brought it up. And if what you’re mentioning is
negative like, “I know what you’re thinking. Maybe this costs a lot of
money.” And they will start thinking, “Oh, this costs a lot of money.”
Not what you want someone to think, right? It puts them out of the
state of desire and into the state of decision. We don’t want to do that.
The other thing is that you risk offense by suggesting or even slightly
suggesting that you can read their minds, that you know them better
than they know themselves. “Look, I know what you’re thinking. I
know you that well.” Well, you don’t, and it can be offensive.
Now, there’s an easy way around that, and that is the may or
technique. So, you want to feel free to use this and combine it with the
action drop technique that I just shared with you. I’m going to give you
some examples here. “I know you may be thinking, but I’ve seen this
already before,” or “I’ve already seen this before.”
Now, look what I’ve done with the tweak of this, with the wordsmithing.
“Now.” Action drop, right? “Now you may be thinking. You may be
thinking.” Notice the word may. “You may be thinking that you’ve
heard this before, or perhaps you already know that this is something
totally new and different.”
Do you see what I’ve done here? First of all, I’ve included the action
drop, and then I said, “You may be thinking this,” which could be a
legitimate thing that you want to bring up.
Then you say, “Or perhaps you already know.” And that really pulls
the user into a unique state. It’s like, “You know, yeah, I probably
should already know that this is something totally new and different.”
And if you didn’t know that by now, the fact that you just told them that
this is something totally new and different, using some pretty powerful
language, it’s going to put them into that state.
So, a far more powerful technique. Avoid the assumption and go with
the may/or technique.

-- 4 of 12 --

5
Okay, we’re going to review the 5-step VSL process in order, so we
can go through all 5 steps, and then we’ll dive into steps one and 2.
Again, step one is the snap suggestion. Step 2 is the vital connection.
Step 3 is the big problem. Step 4, the bigger solution. And step 5, the
grand offer. The 5 steps of any winning video sales letter.
So, let’s begin with step one, the snap suggestion.
This snaps the viewer to attention with the use of a pattern interrupt
and the use of a suggestion. That’s why it’s called the snap
suggestion. The word snap is used because it’s very similar to how
you would feel if you walked into a room and snapped your fingers in
the middle of a conversation. It gets people’s attention.
Now, this is the most creative element in a video sales letter. And I
think it’s even more creative than coming up with a nightmare story, for
example. The most creative element in a video sales letter. So, a lot
of people are kind of like a little afraid of this. They’re saying, “Oh,
maybe I’m not creative enough to come up with something so clever.”
Yes, you are, because we figured out a formula, and it’s pretty easy.
You’ll be impressed. I think you’ll be impressed with yourself.
Here’s how the formula works. This involves an element from your
nightmare story or one of your 3 tips. So, you have 2 choices on
where to grab your creative flow from. Either your nightmare story,
which is almost always where I go for my pattern interrupts, or one of
your 3 tips, if you want to be a little more subdued. Because when you
go with the nightmare story, you can get really kind of crazy. And
that’s good. Pattern interrupts are supposed to be a little bit bizarre.
Now, you can use images, and that’s called an image interrupt, or you
can use words alone, and that’s called a linguistic or verbal interrupt.
It’s up to you. I’ve used both and both work.
Here is the snap suggestion formula. You’re going to want to write this
down.
You start with an optional personal introduction. You have a pattern
interrupt. You have a big promise. You provide video scarcity,
something completely different, and then let’s-make-a-deal transition.
And we’re going to cover each of these in detail, so there’s no
questions.
Okay, the optional introduction. This is the simplest part.
If you are the person behind the product or service, you should
consider using this. In other words, if you are a guy or a gal who wrote
a book, created a video, is the president of a service-related company

-- 5 of 12 --

6
and you want to address your viewers as the president and CEO of
that company, this is something that’s very important to do.
Don’t use this, however, if you’re selling something where you’re not
the face of the product or service that you’re offering. In other words, if
you’re just doing this as a marketing director a big company, this
probably will not work well for you.
And it is simple as this, there’s nothing more to this: “Hi, I’m Jon.” I
usually don’t give my last name. I’ll introduce myself again later in the
video. We’ll cover where to do that. But I’ll just say, “Hi, I’m Jon.” So,
I’ll either say, “Hi” or “Hey, I’m Jon. And that’s it. Your first name. It’s
as simple as that. So that’s your optional introduction.
Then we get into the pattern interrupt, and here’s where things get fun.
A pattern interrupt disengages your viewers mind momentarily, and
grabs their attention via curiosity. And I want you to think of snapping
your fingers during a conversation.
So, picture this. Walk up to a group of friends that you know hopefully
really well, and they’re having a conversation, and you just walk up into
the middle of the conversation and go [snap]. What’s going to happen
in that conversation, if you snap loud enough? Everyone will stop and
they will look at you like you have lost your mind.
So, I’m not suggesting that you go do this, per se, just imagine yourself
doing this.
Well, that is why this is called the snap suggestion. A pattern interrupt
works like a snap. It works like snapping your fingers in a
conversation.
People are arriving at your website expecting to see something. Let’s
say that your product is a service-related product that deals with dry
cleaning. So naturally, when they get to the site, they’re going to see
an image. They’re expecting to see a dry cleaning image.
If you’ve got a weight loss product, they’re expecting to see someone
with abs, or somebody holding out their jeans, or some sort of a weight
loss image. A girl with a scale, whatever.
So here’s what we do. We do not want to give them what they expect.
And when we do not give them what they expect, we break their
pattern.
See, your viewer has a pattern. And when they are too comfortable in
a pattern, they are too comfortable with rejecting the offer. Pattern

-- 6 of 12 --

7
interrupts help people become more accepting of the offer. And it
builds immense curiosity. Again, we want to think forward-thinking
through the entire video sales letter process. You want to keep
thinking forward. Push the viewer forward.
So, if you start off with something very intriguing, very curious, very out
there a little bit, you’re going to have the viewer going, “Wait, I don’t
know, that’s interesting. I want to see where they’re going with this.”
It’s like a plot in a good movie, except we’ve made it much more simple
to do.
Now, I’m going to cover some examples of pattern interrupts. These
are fun. And then, we’re going to cover the anatomy of creating a
pattern interrupt for yourself.
Here’s a pattern interrupt image example. “Hi, I’m Jon,” I use the
optional introduction, “and this is a fish.”
If someone is coming to a weight loss site, the last thing they’re
probably expecting to see is a fish, especially a rainbow-colored fish,
like this one, right?
The next slide says, “And in a few minutes you’ll find out why this fish
is the secret to your weight loss.”
So, at this point they’re going, “What? Am I supposed to eat fish?
Why is this fish so important? I don’t get it.” But I’ve completely
interrupted their pattern.
Remember the old vinyl records? I’ve taken the vinyl record that
they’re playing in their brain, and just scratched it into oblivion. Okay?
They are not expecting this on a diet site, and I’m using a weight loss
example just because that’s the industry that I primarily work in. You
can use this for any industry. It does not matter.
Notice, on this slide, I’m also using the subliminal highlighting
technique that you will find in the 3X VSL Toolbox video. So, I pull out
a certain word, “secret.” It’s a very good word to use, that you highlight
in red, and the subconscious mind will pick that up.
So, I’ve not only broken a pattern here, but I’ve set them up to expect
something in the near future. “In a few minutes, you’re going to find
out why this fish is the secret to your weight loss.”
Here’s another example. “Hi, I’m Jon, and this is my magic jacket.”
So, what do I mean by magic jacket? Well, no one knows yet, right?
So, I go on. “This jacket is unique. And in a few minutes, this jacket

-- 7 of 12 --

8
will show you the secret to losing weight.” It’s almost the same
wording as the fish example. All I added was, “this jacket is unique.”
I want you to notice something here. I want you to notice the words
that I made red. Again, I’m using the subliminal highlighting technique.
It says, “unique secret.” When I make 2 words in one slide red, the
brain has a tendency to look at those 2 words and form a sentence.
So, if you can do that with like “best buy” or “special offer,” and it
makes sense, do it. This case, it worked out perfect. “Unique secret.
This is a unique secret.”
But what I’m really saying is the jacket is unique. “And in a few
minutes, this jacket – it’s a magical jacket – is going to show you the
secret to losing weight.”
So, at this point people are like, now they’re really like, they’re either
confused, they’re puzzled and that’s good because I’ve broken their
pattern. But they are intrigued, they are curious. “Where is he going
with this?”
Now, you’re probably wondering where I’m going with this too, and I’m
going to explain how I created these pattern interrupts and how you
can do the exact same thing, in just a moment.
This is a linguistic interrupt with subliminal highlighting. And my
shocking statement here is the phrase “magic pill answer.” If you use
a linguistic interrupt, you’re going to want to use something pretty
much over the top, pretty shocking.
So I say, “Hi, I’m Jon. In just a few minutes, you’ll discover the magic
pill answer to your weight loss goals.”
Now, the irony here is I would go on to explain that the magic pill is
actually something that you create yourself. It’s not something you
swallow. I just call it the magic pill answer. It’s the solution to believing
that you have to take a magic pill. You don’t. There are no magic pills.
So, the answer is there are no magic pills. I go into that later in the
video.
But right now, I’ve captured their attention big-time, with that pattern
interrupt. “A magic pill answer to weight loss? What? I don’t even
know what you’re talking about.”
Those are some samples of pattern interrupts.
Now, I want to show you the anatomy of a pattern interrupt, so that you
can build one yourself.

-- 8 of 12 --

9
The image interrupt is using a nightmare story. I prefer using
nightmare stories. I’m going to show you both, a nightmare story and
tips, as well. But I prefer using the nightmare story to create an image
interrupt. Remember the fish and the jacket? Those are image
interrupts, and both of them, by the way, used my nightmare story; 2
different nightmare stories, because I gave you 2 different examples.
Here’s how you do it. Step one: write down 5 objects that are
referenced in your nightmare story, and that have nothing to do with
your product or service that you’re selling. Do I sell jackets? No. Do I
sell fish? No. Okay? That’s what makes it a pattern interrupt.
If I showed them a picture, a bottle of a supplement, a supplement
bottle, or a scale, or anything like that, that’s too close to what I do.
That’s too close to what I do.
So, I’m going to show you how I literally did this in my own nightmare
story. I picked 5 objects that have nothing to do, nothing to do with my
product.
So, in my nightmare story – if you do not remember the story, go back.
It’s in the video sections. Go back and watch the video on creating
your nightmare story. I give the entire thing. You can download the
transcript, if you want. But I’ll briefly cover the highlights of it, if you
don’t remember. You probably do, because it was a pretty cool story.
I was leaving work in a car. It was cold outside. I left my coat in the
office, and we ended up at a convenience store. We drove by
prostitutes and I stole donuts. Okay? That’s the long and short of it.
So, I’m going to take 5 objects from my nightmare story. Okay, what
are 5 objects from what I just said? Well, a car; a jacket – it’s where I
hid my donuts, in my jacket; prostitutes – probably not the best choice;
a store, in this case a convenience store; and a donut.
So, why did I choose a jacket? Well I could have chosen anyone of
those, but I chose a jacket because it doesn’t have anything to do with
weight loss – that’s my product – yet it connects directly to my
nightmare story.
That jacket was how I snuck out the donuts. I snuck the donuts by
hiding them in my jacket and down my pants, at the same time. I
snuck out 3 or 4 boxes. Like I said, they weren’t really donuts, but they
were close enough to being donuts.
So, the jacket ties perfectly into my nightmare story.
So, when I get to my nightmare story element, that whole loop that I

-- 9 of 12 --

1
0
just opened with a pattern interrupt, because they’re just dying to know
how that relates, is closed in the nightmare story. I’ll reference it.
I go, “Remember that magic jacket I told you about? Well, this is why
that jacket was magical for me and why it’s going to tell you everything
you need to know.” I could go on about the jacket. “That jacket
caused me to do something horrible, but that jacket was also
something that caused me to change my life.” So, I go into it like that.
Now, I did not use a donut, okay? It’s too close to food and dieting,
although it could have worked with some creative writing. I’m not
saying that it couldn’t work. But I wanted to veer away from the donut
because, remember, people are hitting a diet site. I’m wanting them to
see what they do not expect. So, a donut may be a little to close.
Although, a donut on a diet site is a tad shocking, so with creative
writing, it would have worked.
So, that’s step 2, select the object.
And then step 3 is to reference your object to the number one desire of
your reader. Okay? Reference the object to the number one desire of
your reader. In my case, it’s weight loss. In your case, it could be
anything. It could be paper shredding, dry cleaning, I want to ski
better, I want to make more money online, whatever their desire is, you
reference it to their desire.
Here’s an example. “This is a jacket, and this jacket is the secret to
your weight loss,” or “This jacket holds the secret to your weight loss.”
Or, “In a few minutes, I’ll reveal how this jacket will give you the secret
to your weight loss.”
It has to be their weight loss, not your weight loss. It has to be they’re
skiing better. It has to be them making money online, them
succeeding in whatever product or service that you’re offering.
So, all I’ve done is take the object that has nothing to do with the
primary thing that I’m selling, and I relate it back to the primary desire
of my reader. I don’t relate it to what I’m selling. So, if I’m selling a
book on weight loss, I don’t say, “And this jacket will show you why you
should buy Every Other Day Diet.” That’s not a pattern interrupt.
That’s a suicide mission waiting to happen. You don’t want to do that.
But, notice how the jacket ties directly to weight loss. But you don’t
explain how. You explain how later, and your nightmare story helps
explain how.
Now, this builds intense curiosity, creates a mental pattern interrupt,
and it sets your viewer up for a specific suggestion.

-- 10 of 12 --

1
1
When your viewer is placed in a pattern interrupt, he or she is much
more apt to have a suggestion implanted in their minds. This is why
hypnosis works, by the way. Hypnosis is very similar to this. Hypnosis
takes you into a certain state, and in a certain state you are much more
open to having a suggestion given to you. Bark like a dog, and people
bark like a dog.
You’re not going to ask your viewer to bark like a dog, you’re going to
ask them a simple thing to do. You’re going to ask them to do
something very simple, and I’ll show you what that is in just a few
minutes.
Now, here’s a pattern interrupt. Before I go over that, I want to go over
the verbal interrupt, because I promised that I would.
So step one, here’s how you build the verbal interrupt. This is where
you want to go back to your driving force behind your prime benefit and
your USP.
Before I go any further now you see why you were doing all the USP
homework before. Now you see why you had to pull out your prime
benefit. What’s the most unique thing that your product or service
solves for your reader or your client? The most unique thing. That’s
your prime benefit.
Now, how does that unique thing work? Well, it’s the driving force.
That’s the driving force. I’m going to give you an example, using my
own, so it will refresh your memory. But this is why it’s so important.
So using the driving force behind your prime benefit and your USP,
you want to write down the primary thing your viewer will discover
while watching your video sales letter. That’s it. What’s the primary
thing they’re going to discover?
Here’s an example. This is write out of my Every Other Day Diet video
that started this whole thing. “You’re about to discover one really old
and kind of weird fat loss trick that I’ve used for years.” This tip
references my prime benefit. If you will recall, my prime benefit was
the ability to eat your favorite foods and lose weight. It’s referencing
that tip, but it specifically references that tip’s driving force.
“Why does that work? How can you eat your favorite foods and still
lose weight? Well, the reason why it works is because you stagger
your calories. You eat fewer calories for a few days, and then on the
days where you eat more calories, you can consume your favorite
foods like pizza.” It works, okay?
So, that is the driving force, staggering calories.

-- 11 of 12 --

1
2
Now, all I’ve done is take that and worded it in an obscure way, worded
it in a little cloak and dagger. “You’re about to discover” – again, a list
of power words and phrases are in the download section. “You’re
about to discover one really old and kind of weird fat loss trick.” It is
kind of weird. It’s kind of weird to say, “You shouldn’t eat the same
amount of food every day.” In fact, some days you should eat ice
cream. That’s kind of weird.
The reason I say it’s kind of old is because human beings have been
doing this for eons. And for eons, 3 squares didn’t happen until the
industrial revolution. It was unheard of. People were always eating
more and less, more and less, and obesity was practically unheard of.
So, it was only when people can gorge themselves and they eat too
frequently that obesity happens.
I’m telling you this so you can understand how I worded that sentence.
So, all you have to do for yourself is think about your driving force
behind your prime benefit. And then, take that driving force and relate
it back to what the viewer will discover.
So, inside the video, they’re going to discover blank. It can be 3 tips
for this, it can they’ll discover the solution to blank, the solution to dry
cleaning, the solution to paper shredding, the solution to being the best
barefoot skier in the world. Whatever it is, it doesn’t matter. The
solution to your legal issues. It can be anything.
“You’re about to discover” blank, and that’s it. This is what you’re
about to discover. And then if you want to wordsmith it, you can say
something like, “A very unique and extremely unusual, one-of-a-kind,”
blank.”
So, this is how you wordsmith things.
That is a verbal pattern interrupt. It’s not as flashy and fun as the
picture pattern interrupt, but it certainly works well.
Now, this can also be expanded out over one to 3 slides. So, an
example with that would be, “You’re about to discover one really old
and kind of weird fat loss trick. I’ve used it for years. In fact, this tip
has been passed around to over 300,000 people. It’s helped hundreds
of thousands of people lose weight for 10 years now.”
So, I just expand it a little bit. You can expand it out over one to 3
slides, if you wish, or you can just keep it at one.

-- 12 of 12 --
